passage:
Under Williams v. Asplundh Tree Expert Co., No. 3:05-cv-00479-VMC-MCR (M.D. Fla. July 23, 2013), 
  general objections are not useful and will not be considered by the Court. Objections should be  specific and 
  supported by a detailed explanation.
citation_text_in_passage: Williams v. Asplundh Tree Expert Co., No. 3:05-cv-00479-VMC-MCR (M.D. Fla. July 23, 2013)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Williams v. Asplundh Tree Expert is a real civil rights case, but it does not deal with 
  objects or contain any support for the proposition that objections should be specific and supported by a detailed 
  explanation. In the original citation, the litigant also made errors in the citation format. To keep things focused
  on mischaracterization identification, I have corrected those citation errors in the passage bove.

cited_case.case_name: Williams v. Asplundh Tree Expert Co.

passage:
United States v. Baker, 529 Fed. Appx. 987 (11th Cir. 2013), confirms broad discovery rights under 
  Rules 26 and 30.
citation_text_in_passage: United States v. Baker, 529 Fed. Appx. 987 (11th Cir. 2013)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
United States v. Baker is a real 11th Circuit case from 2013, but it is an appeal challenging 
  a criminal's sentencing enhancement. While Rules 26 and 30 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure do establish 
  broad discovery rights, United States v. Baker is an appeal of a criminal's sentencing enhancement and does not 
  relate to discovery. The litigant also used incorrect numbering in the citation, but to keep the focus on 
  mischaracterizations, I corrected those in the passage. NOTE: I slightly changed the wording of the passage here. 
  The quote in the order is just 'confirms broad discovery rights under Rules 26 and 30.' Also note that the passage 
  contains an incorrect citation.

passage:
AFRH fails to identify any 'actual problem' necessitating this sweeping ban, relying instead on ipse 
  dixit assertions that passive political expression is disruptive. [citation omitted]. But the Supreme Court has 
  unequivocally foreclosed that argument, holding that the wearing of political slogans is 'nondisruptive by nature.' 
  Minnesota Voters All. v. Mansky, 585 U.S. 1, 19 (2018).
citation_text_in_passage: Minnesota Voters All. v. Mansky, 585 U.S. 1, 19 (2018)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
This one is a direct quote. Mansky is a real US Supreme Court case and the citation is correct. 
  However, the SCOTUS did not use the phrase 'nondisruptive by nature' and noted that wearing passive political 
  apparel could be disruptive depending on the setting.

passage:
Communications by independent third parties cannot be attributed to defendants absent clear evidence 
  of an agency relationship. Haider v. Lyft, Inc., No. 20 Civ. 2997 (AJN), 2021 WL 3475621 (S.D.N.Y. Aug. 6, 2021).
citation_text_in_passage: Haider v. Lyft, Inc.
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
This passage indicates that both the OConner and Haider cases were misrepresented. However, for
  ease, I tweaked this to just cite Haider. Haider has nothing to do with agency law and does not discuss when a 
  third party's communications can be attributed to defendants.

passage:
The 10th Circuit recognized in Capital Solutions, LLC v. Konica Minolta Business Solutions USA, Inc., 
  695 F.Supp.2d 1149, 1154-56 (10th Cir. 2010), that the jury's determination on this issue [i.e., the amount of punitive damages] 
  is entitled to finality. [Id. at 1].
citation_text_in_passage:
Capital Solutions, LLC v. Konica Minolta Business Solutions USA, Inc., 695 F.Supp.2d 
  1149, 1154-56 (10th Cir. 2010)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
This one contains two mischaracterizations: 1) it characterizes the case as a 10th Circuit case
  when in fact it was a district court decision, and 2) it erroneously cites the case in support of the proposition 
  that a jury's determination of the amount of punitive damages is 'entitled to finality' under the Reexamination Clause.

cited_case.case_name: Cap. Sols., LLC v. Konica Minolta Bus. Sols. U.S.A., Inc.

passage:
S.M. v. J.M., 811 A.2d 621, 625 (Pa. Super. 2002) supports the proposition that failing to remedy 
  alienation and pattern of obstruction would 'irreparably harm' child.
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
I added 'S.M. v. J.M., 811 A.2d 621, 625 (Pa. Super. 2002) supports the proposition that' 
  to the beginning so it is clear what's being cited. This is a real case but it does not discuss parental alienation
  or use the phrase 'irreparably harm'.

passage:
The First Circuit has repeatedly emphasized that a district court abuses its discretion when it 
  fails to articulate any rationale for denying a Rule 59(e) motion. See Davila-Alvarez v. Escuela de Medicina 
  Universidad Cent. del Caribe, 257 F.3d 58, 63-64 (1st Cir. 2001).
citation_text_in_passage:
Davila-Alvarez v. Escuela de Medicina Universidad Cent. del Caribe, 257 F.3d 58, 
  63-64 (1st Cir. 2001).
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
'repeatedly emphasized that a district court abuses its discretion when it fails to articulate 
  any rationale for denying a Rule 59(e) motion' is a direct but partial quote from the court decision. I added 
  'The First Circuit has'. Davila-Alvarez does not even involve a 59(e) motion. It involves motions for relief from 
  judgment under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 60(b) in the context of a dismissal for want of prosecution, and the 
  court states it has in fact denied Rule 59(e) motions without articulating a rationale.

cited_case.case_name: Davila-Alvarez v. Escuela de Medicina Universidad Cent. del Caribe

passage:
Orders entered by a disqualified judge are voidable and must be vacated upon proper motion. See
  Fischer v. Knuck, 497 So. 2d 240 (Fla. 1986).
citation_text_in_passage: Fischer v. Knuck, 497 So. 2d 240 (Fla. 1986)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
This one is a direct quote from the filing, but the filing also cites MacKenzie v. Super Kids 
  Bargain Store, Inc., 565 So. 2d 1332 (Fla. 1990). For clarity, I am limiting examples to only one cited case. Court

passage:
State v. Monfort, 723 N.E.2d 407 (Ind. 2000) struck down a statute giving prosecutors veto power over

passage:
McCary v. Commonwealth, 228 Va. 219, 231 (1984) holds that probable cause must appear within the four 
  corners of the written affidavit or recorded testimony presented to the magistrate.
citation_text_in_passage: McCary v. Commonwealth, 228 Va. 219, 231 (1984)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
This is one I had to somewhat reconstruct from the court's ruling. The following is a direct
  quote from the brief: 'probable cause must appear within the four corners of the written affidavit or recorded 
  testimony presented to the magistrate'. However, I added 'McCary v. Commonwealth, 228 Va. 219, 231 (1984) holds that'
  to the beginning. Also, the court seems to suggest that the brief cited this language as a direct quote from McCary,
  but since I am unable to confirm, to be conservative I have not put the language in quotation marks in the passage.

passage:
Anderson v. Commonwealth, 38 Va. App. 321 (2002), aff'd, 279 Va. 85 (2010) supports the proposition
  that in camera review [is required] once materiality is asserted.
citation_text_in_passage: Anderson v. Commonwealth, 38 Va. App. 321 (2002), aff'd, 279 Va. 85 (2010)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
I took the direct quote from the brief that was included in the opinion ('supports the proposition
  that in camera review [is required] once materiality is asserted') and added the full citation to the beginning.
  38 Va. App. 321 (2002) is the citation for Thomas v. Commonwealth, a different case. Thomas does not support the 
  assertion that 'in camera review is required once materiality is asserted.' Anderson v. Commonwealth does not support
  that assertion either.

passage:
Complex pro se pleadings should not be dismissed merely for length where facts are discernible.
  Rinehart v. Weitzell, 964 F.3d 684, 687 (8th Cir. 2020).
citation_text_in_passage: Rinehart v. Weitzell, 964 F.3d 684, 687 (8th Cir. 2020)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
This one was pulled from footnote 24 of the show cause order. Rinehart does not hold or imply
  this.

passage:
Courts have discretion to deny leave to amend where a plaintiff fails to cure deficiencies in their 
  complaint. Alvarez v. City of Chicago, 605 F.3d 445, 452-53 (7th Cir. 2010).
citation_text_in_passage: Alvarez v. City of Chicago, 605 F.3d 445, 452-53 (7th Cir. 2010).
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
The wording is from the court decision, not a direct quote from the brief. Alvarez is a real
  case, but the pincite is wrong because Alvarez ends at 451 and it says nothing about the circumstances under which 
  a court may dismiss a complaint with prejudice.

passage:
Voluntary engagement with a motion waives procedural objections not timely raised. Bittinger v. 
  Tecumseh Products Co., 123 F.3d 877, 881 (6th Cir. 1997).
citation_text_in_passage: Bittinger v. Tecumseh Products Co., 123 F.3d 877, 881 (6th Cir. 1997).
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Reconstructed from court order. Bittinger is an ERISA and res judicata case discussing privity, 
  virtual representation, and class-related issues and does not appear to support the waiver proposition for which 
  it is cited.

passage:
Pincus v. American Traffic Solutions, Inc., 25 F.4th 1339 (11th Cir. 2022) (per curiam), reaffirmed 
  that Florida law bars unjust enrichment or other equitable recovery where the payer knowingly elects to pay rather 
  than pursue available alternatives.
citation_text_in_passage: Pincus v. American Traffic Solutions, Inc., 25 F.4th 1339 (11th Cir. 2022) (per curiam)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Direct brief quote sourced from the show cause order, with citation added. Pincus affirmed 
  dismissal of an unjust enrichment claim 'because [the defendant] had given [the plaintiff] value in exchange for 
  the fee,' and thus 'it was not inequitable for [the defendant] to retain the fee,' not because of the voluntary 
  payment doctrine.

cited_case.case_name: Pincus v. American Traffic Solutions, Inc.

passage:
Weseman v. Wells Fargo Home Mortg., No. 06-1338, 2008 WL542961 (D. Or. Feb. 22, 2008) stands for the 
  proposition that even where disputes are not directed to the consumer reporting agency itself, if a complaint 
  provide sufficient detail and is transmitted through a known channel (i.e CFPB), obligations to reinvestigate may be triggered.
citation_text_in_passage: Weseman v. Wells Fargo Home Mortg., No. 06-1338, 2008 WL542961 (D. Or. Feb. 22, 2008)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Weseman analyzed the doctrine of preemption and conflicts between common law and other FCRA 
  provisions. It did not discuss direct disputes and statutory obligations for a consumer reporting agency as the 
  defendant in that case was a furnisher, not a consumer reporting agency.

cited_case.case_name: Weseman v. Wells Fargo Home Mortg., No. 06-1338

passage:

passage:
In Khan v. GBAK Properties, Inc., 371 S.W.3d 347 (Tex. App.—Houston [1st Dist.] 2012, no pet.) the court 
  reversed summary judgment and held that a lender's failure to strictly comply with the statutory notice provision 
  prevented lawful foreclosure.
citation_text_in_passage: Khan v. GBAK Properties, Inc., 371 S.W.3d 347 (Tex. App.—Houston [1st Dist.] 2012, no pet.)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
'the court reversed summary judgment...' is a direct quote. I added the citation. The issues and
  holding of Khan concern the statute of limitations for foreclosure, whether an acceleration of a note was ever 
  abandoned, and evidence of tortious interference. While Khan did reverse a summary judgment, the reversal was due 
  to a statute of limitations issue and not one of notice.

A stay of discovery is an extraordinary remedy and the movant bears the burden of showing 'good cause'

passage:
The Ninth Circuit has recognized that pro se litigants may recover reasonable costs for necessary

citation_text_in_passage: Shum v. Intel Corp., 629 F.3d 1360, 1367 (Fed. Cir. 2010)

passage:
The United States Judicial Panel on Multidistrict Litigation has denied transfer where plaintiff's

In re Eliquis (Apixaban) Products Liability Litigation, 282 F. Supp. 3d 1354, 1356 
  (J.P.M.L. 2017)

cited_case.case_name: In re Eliquis (Apixaban) Products Liability Litigation

passage:
In Pace v Raisman & Assocs., Esqs, LLP, (95 AD3d 1185 [2d Dept 2012]), the client waited until after 
  the attorney demanded unpaid legal fees before raising counterclaims and grievances that had never been mentioned 
  during the representation. The Second Department dismissed the counterclaims, emphasizing that claims raised only 
  after the breakdown of the attorney-client relationship and in response to fee demands lack credibility and legal 
  merit.
citation_text_in_passage: Pace v Raisman & Assocs., Esqs, LLP, (95 AD3d 1185 [2d Dept 2012])
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Added 'In Pace v Raisman & Assocs., Esqs, LLP, (95 AD3d 1185 [2d Dept 2012])'. Everything else
  is a direct brief quote. In Pace, the plaintiff sued his former attorney's alleging causes of action sounding in 
  (among other things) legal malpractice and fraud. The Second Department found that the malpractice claim must be 
  dismissed under CPLR 3211 (a)(5) as it was time-barred and that the fraud claim must be dismissed for failing to be 
  pied with the requisite particularity required under CPLR 306(b) and because it was duplicative of the malpractice 
  claim (Pace, 95 AD3d at 118). The case does not involve counterclaims or unpaid legal fees.

cited_case.case_name: Pace v Raisman & Assocs., Esqs, LLP

passage:
In Butler v Catinella, (58 AD3d 145 [2d Dept 2008]), the Second Department sanctioned a party for 
  verified allegations refuted by documentary evidence.
citation_text_in_passage: Butler v Catinella, (58 AD3d 145 [2d Dept 2008])
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Added the case citation to the beginning. Everything else is a direct brief quote. In Butler, 
  the court ruled that the defense of failure to state a cause of action was a proper affirmative defense that could 
  be asserted in an answer and that certain other counterclaims and affirmative defenses in the answer must be 
  dismissed as lacking merit. The Butler case does not involve sanctions.

passage:
In VisionChina Media Inc. v Shareholder Representative Servs., LLC (109 AD3d 49 [2d Dept 2013]), 
  the court granted attachment to prevent post-litigation dissipation.
citation_text_in_passage: VisionChina Media Inc. v Shareholder Representative Servs., LLC (109 AD3d 49 [2d Dept 2013])
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Added the case citation to the beginning. Everything else is a direct brief quote. In VisionChina
  Media Inc., the First Department did the exact opposite of what Mr. Kleyman claims and reversed the lower court's 
  order granting the plaintiff's motion to attach the defendant's assets.

cited_case.case_name: VisionChina Media Inc. v Shareholder Representative Servs., LLC

passage:
CPLR 6201(1) aims to prevent defendants from rendering judgments ineffectual by removing assets from 
  jurisdictional reach. Erdman Anthony & Assoc., Inc. v Barkstrom (298 AD2d 981 [4th Dept 2002]).
citation_text_in_passage: Erdman Anthony & Assoc., Inc. v Barkstrom (298 AD2d 981 [4th Dept 2002])
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Added the reference to CPLR 6201(1) to the beginning. Everything else is a direct brief quote. 
  Erdman involves the granting of the defendant's motion for summary judgment dismissing an account stated cause of 
  action and does not involve prejudgment attachment orders.

cited_case.case_name: Erdman Anthony & Assoc., Inc. v Barkstrom

passage:
A defendant's partial payments, made voluntarily without reservation of rights or protest, constitute a 
  ratification of plaintiff's invoices and services rendered. Gould v Decolotor, (121 AD3d 845 [2d Dept 2014]).
citation_text_in_passage: Gould v Decolotor, (121 AD3d 845 [2d Dept 2014])
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Direct brief quote with the citation added. The Second Department held in Gould that the 
  defendant failed to establish that the plaintiff's claim for breach of an implied contract for legal services under 
  a quantum meruit theory was time-barred. The court further held that the allegations in plaintiff's complaint were 
  insufficient to support a claim of fraud. In addition, the court held that plaintiff's claim for a contingency fee 
  'was dismissible on the ground that the plaintiff failed to provide the defendants with a writing identifying the 
  method by which the contingency fee was to be determined in violation of former Code of Professional Responsibility 
  DR 2-106 [d] [22 NYCRR 1200.11 [d)'. Finally, the court found that plaintiff's claim for punitive damages must be 
  dismissed. Thus, Gould did not involve partial payments made voluntarily without reservation of rights or protest.

passage:
Rule 11 may apply to pro se parties, but courts must take into account a pro se litigant's lack of legal 
  training. Warren v. Guelker, 29 F.3d 1386, 1390 (9th Cir. 1994).
citation_text_in_passage: Warren v. Guelker, 29 F.3d 1386, 1390 (9th Cir. 1994)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Direct brief quote from court decision. Warren contains no such holding. In fact, its holding 
  is that courts cannot decline to impose sanctions under Rule 11 simply based on a litigant's pro se status.

passage:
As the Supreme Court has cautioned, courts must avoid using Rule 11 as a bar to the courthouse door for 
  litigants attempting in good faith to remedy non-malicious oversights. Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp., 496 U.S. 384 
  (1990).
citation_text_in_passage: Cooter & Gell v. Hartmarx Corp., 496 U.S. 384 (1990)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes: Direct brief quote from court decision. Cooter contains no such holding.

passage:
Prejudice to defendants is not a valid ground to deny leave where amendment would correct critical 
  defects in the original pleading. Doe v. McMillan, 566 F.2d 713, 720 (D.C. Cir. 1977)
citation_text_in_passage: Doe v. McMillan, 566 F.2d 713, 720 (D.C. Cir. 1977)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Direct brief quote from a footnote in the court decision. The relevant portion of that case 
  stands for the opposite proposition: The district court's denial of leave to amend on prejudice alone was not an 
  abuse of discretion.

passage: Courts routinely grant leave even at later stages when the amendment serves justice and is not futile.
citation_text_in_passage: Richardson v. United States, 193 F.3d 545, 548 (D.C. Cir. 1999)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Direct brief quote from a footnote in the court decision. Richardson involved a district court's 
  denial of leave to amend after dismissing a complaint at the motion-to-dismiss posture, not a “later stage,” and the 
  circuit explicitly did not review or reach the denial of leave to amend.

passage:
Government-imposed passport restrictions must be justified by compelling evidence. Haig v. Agee,

The case actually says that 'a consistent administration construction of th[e] statute must be 
  followed by the courts 'unless there are compelling indications that it is wrong (quoting E. I. du Pont de Nemours

passage:
Corporate subsidiaries and their parent entities may be treated as a single legal entity. Harris v. One Hope

citation_text_in_passage: Harris v. One Hope United, Inc., 28 N.E.3d 804 (Ill. 2015)

cited_case.case_name: Harris v. One Hope United, Inc.

passage:
Where a party benefits unjustly from another's reliance in a trust-based relationship, Alabama law
  permits recovery. In Jordan v. Mitchell 705 So.2d 453 (Ala.Civ. App.1997), the court recognized that a confidential,
  relationship may support equitable relief even when a formal contract exists.
citation_text_in_passage: Jordan v. Mitchell 705 So.2d 453 (Ala.Civ. App.1997)
label: mischaracterized
annotator_notes:
Direct quote pulled from the court opinion. No contract was at issue in Jordan, which discussed 
  the 'imposition of a constructive trust or quasi-contract where improvements to property were made by a party not 
  the owner of the property.' The Jordan court did analyze whether there had been abuse of a confidential relationship
  such that a constructive trust should be imposed. And it did mention that the plaintiff 'might have been entitled 
  to relief if she had alleged and proved that [the defendant] was guilty of some wrongdoing in inducing her to make 
  the improvements to the property.' Nevertheless, the Jordan court did not discuss whether Alabama law permits 
  equitable relief in the presence of a formal contract.

passage:

  the prima facie case.'

passage:
Intentional torts such as false arrest and malicious prosecution fall outside the protection of the 
  Indiana Tort Claims Act. Wilson v. Isaacs, 929 N.E.2d 200, 203-04 (Ind. 2010).
citation_text_in_passage: Wilson v. Isaacs, 929 N.E.2d 200, 203-04 (Ind. 2010)
label: accurate
annotator_notes:
Direct brief quote, except I altered the end to write out the full name of the act rather than
  'ITCA'. Wilson quotes the ITCA as stating that a government entity or employee is not liable if a loss results 
  from the enforcement of a law unless the act of enforcement constitutes false arrest or false imprisonment.

passage:
The Fifth Circuit has established clear standards for when spoliation sanctions are appropriate, 
  requiring proof that the party acted in bad faith - mere negligence is insufficient. King v. Illinois Cent. R.R., 
  337 F.3d 550, 556 (5th Cir. 2003).
citation_text_in_passage: King v. Illinois Cent. R.R., 337 F.3d 550, 556 (5th Cir. 2003)
label: accurate
annotator_notes:
Direct brief quote. King held that a movant must show bad faith to establish that it was 
  entitled to an adverse inference based on destruction of evidence.
